speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you were successful in the fashion industry when you became active in the Simon Wiesenthal Center and became a public speaker on the Holocaust. And then you said that you broke away from the fashion industry and quit your business because they were not happy about your speaking on the Holocaust. I wonder if you could explain that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had a conflict with it. I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a conflict with it. I had to be all the time on in the fashion industry, and I did fashion shows, and it just, I don't know, it just became trivial. And I had a hard time putting myself into-- from one mood to the other. And so I sold my business. For a while, I was teaching a fashion class at UCLA. And then when Rabbi Cooper asked me to come to the Simon Wiesenthal Center, he and I were the outreach program. And I realized that it's quite important that we should tell our children about the Holocaust. It was then when they first began-- we first began to hear about revisionists, and then later, Neo-Nazis, and Aryan Nations, and all these anti-Holocaust groups. These groups that deny, not only say that, you know, the Jews are lying but that the Holocaust, of course, never happened. And I realized that maybe that's more important at this time of my life than being glamorous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I know that you have touched and changed literally thousands, maybe tens of thousands, of lives. You were going to so many schools every school year. And this, of course-- this interview-- is being done for the archives, for the future generations. Are there-- is there any message, anything you would like to say to those who might be watching these tapes 20, 30, 50, years from now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I speak to children, first of all, I tell them that I learned one very important lesson from the Holocaust. And that was that I will never judge people collectively. That I learned that each individual human being has to be judged by his own merit-- who he is, what he does, how he acts. I also tell children that they have the power to change the world. That we each and every one of us, by our actions and how we live our lives, are changing the world for better or for the worse. And I hope that my children will follow up, will not forget where they come from. I want to leave this legacy to them. I want them to learn from our past. And I hope that it will never happen again. We are leaving behind 20th century, which was probably the most violent, most brutal, centuries in the history of mankind. And I pray and hope that the 21st century will be better. Not for my sake-- I certainly will  be not-- I will not be here anymore. But for my children's, grandchildren's sake, and for humanity in general. I hope that the world will come to their senses. And that there won't be another Bosnia or another Africa or any of the genocides that occurred in my lifetime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your story is certainly a testament to the brutality that human beings are capable of. And you mentioned some of the individual cases where friends-- or supposed friends-- betrayed you or didn't help you, and the help you didn't get in so many instances. Yet at the same time, you mentioned that your sister-in-law was hidden by Gentiles and saved that way in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was not hidden by Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was hiding with Gentile papers. She was in hiding on her own. She went through terrible times. Both-- I have another sister-in-law who also was hiding in Budapest. And they were terrified every moment when they are going to be captured, because they were stopping people on the street with identifications. And if they-- if they didn't believe that your papers were authentic, you were finished in Budapest. And men had it even worse, because if they felt that the man is not-- that his papers are fake, they just made them take off their pants, the Jewish men who were circumcised, and they immediately took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Europe, most men are not circumcised if they're Christians, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. They weren't. I don't know today. But at that time, they weren't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I sometimes tell the groups that I speak before that I have never met a single survivor who doesn't know at least one, maybe several people, who were saved by Gentiles. Would that hold true for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I happen to be fortunate enough to meet here in Los Angeles a man by the name of John Weidner. I don't know whether you knew him. He was a Dutchman who organized an escape route from Holland, through France, into Spain and into Switzerland. He and his sister, as a matter of fact. And his sister was captured by the Gestapo and died in a concentration camp. And John escaped. And then he was captured again and escaped. And he tells a very interesting story, and I tell it to the children. He said that his father was a Dutch minister who he used to listen to on Sundays, and that his father used to end his sermon by saying, if you save a life, it is as if you saved a whole nation. And he says, I didn't know what my father meant. And then he told a little story, which says that a little boy was praying to God to please look at this world and save the people because they are killing each other. They are hating each other. And the little boy said, please, God. Send someone to help us. And God answered, I did. I sent you. He says, when I first heard what was happening to the Jewish people, he says, I immediately understood what I had to do. And he says, then it was no doubt in my mind that I must help. And it was that simple. I also met a woman by the name of Irene Opdyke, from Poland, who hid, I think, 13 Jews in the Nazi headquarters where she was a maid. I mean, there are amazing stories. The unfortunate thing is that there were very few of those-- the Schindlers the Raoul Wallenbergs-- unfortunately, very few. And I often wonder, and I heard Rabbi Hier one day saying this, and I thought about it, that when they called the Wannsee Conference to arrange for this final solution, if there would have been some Schindlers or some Raoul Wallenbergs among those Nazis, how would have been the Holocaust recorded, what would've been the difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you have some pictures--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes!

speaker: interviewer
text: --that we could look at.

speaker: interviewee
text: I certainly do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that this is the right time to do so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why don't we do that? Renee, you mentioned Bernard, your husband, quite a few times. And I wonder if you'd like to actually introduce him to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the young man I married. His name is Bernard Firestone, as you know. And we've been married, I think, 48 or 49 years. Fighting, loving, we took the whole gamut-- did the whole gamut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to add anything to that, Bernard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mainly fighting. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'm glad to see that all that happened has not deprived you of a sense of humor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I am so glad to see that you still laugh a lot. You're a happy person. You're enjoying life. And most importantly, you have made life so much better for so many thousands of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you!

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Renee.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the advice I give usually young couples. If they fight long enough, they will stay married long enough. [LAUGHS] Thank you, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything other than fighting that you might add to what makes a marriage last as long as yours did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we've--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm--

speaker: interviewee
text: --been working together for many years.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he asked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quite devoted to each other. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course you share two dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: We've just lived a normal family life. We work together. We're in the same business, and we do the same thing all the time. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And also we have spoken quite a great deal about the family you've lost, the people you've known, and some of the pictures that you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the family we have now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Our child, our daughter, and then our granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely. And I think that it would be very nice if you could show us some of the people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly will.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the pictures. Some of the people that you [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just want you to know that my granddaughter adores her grandpa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, is that so unusual?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I don't know. It's not unusual, but I just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I can tell you it isn't. My granddaughter adores her grandpa.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I hope so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let us see some of those pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. In this picture is-- in this picture is my father, Maurice Weinfeld. The picture was taken, I think, in 1918 or 1919. He was in the Austro-Hungarian Army. And I am not sure, but I think it was taken in Ungvar, which was Uzhorod in Czechoslovakia. And this picture was taken in Uzhorod, Czechoslovakia, I think around 1932. On the picture is my mother-- in the middle-- [? Johanna ?] Weinfeld, maiden name Rosenfeld. On the right side is my father, Maurice Weinfeld. And I am on the left side, Renee Firestone now. This picture is really a treasure for me. I recovered it just recently. It's a picture of me at the age of maybe 12. I used to dance on all kinds of social dinners and charity events. So it's hard for me to believe that that's me. It was done in [INAUDIBLE], Czechoslavakia, just before the Hungarians took over the region. Probably around the same year, '32, '34 maybe. This picture was taken about the same time, 1932, '34, between the time, of my little sister, Klara Weinfeld, in Uzhorod, Czechoslovakia, probably performing the same time I was performing. This is again my little sister, Klara. And this picture was taken at the River Uzh, of my hometown, Uzhorod, which was named after that river. And this picture was taken shortly before they took us away from home. She was 14 years old. And she died in Auschwitz, 1938, probably. By the way, I made a mistake on the previous picture of my sister. It was taken probably 1944 just before they took us to camp. This is my brother, Frank Valenta. He changed his name from Weinfeld. And this picture I think was taken in Israel. He went to Israel when I came to the United States. I have no idea where or what year. He still looks very young there. This is me. The picture was taken right after the War, probably in Prague, Czechoslovakia. This is new hair. After my shaved head, this was the first hair that grew out. And, I don't know why, but when I look at this picture, my eyes are crying. Maybe it's just my fantasy. It was taken in 1945. I was here 20, 21. I'm not sure. This is my husband and I. The picture was taken in Prague. It really is not our wedding picture, but since we don't have a wedding picture, this is what we are using as a substitute. The picture was taken in 19-- probably '47. This picture was taken while I was coming down from the plane with my daughter, Klara, who was 11 months old at the time. The picture was taken in 1948, October the 31st, in the LaGuardia Airport. We arrived to New York and then went to Pennsylvania. This is my daughter, Klara. She was named after my little sister who died in Auschwitz. The picture was taken in Los Angeles. I'm not sure, but in the late '70s or early '80s. I'm not sure. This is my granddaughter, Johanna Specter. The picture was taken I think two years ago in 1992 in Los Angeles. This is a family picture that somebody sent me from South America. Where it survived, how it survived, I don't know. The picture was taken in 1932. On the left side, that little girl is me. Next to me is my mother and then my father. And next to it is my father's sister. Berta Yakubovich was her name. And my uncle, Joseph Yakubovich And the bride is their daughter. Her name was Julia, and she was my first cousin. And the groom was also my first cousin. And his name was Dr. Fred Weinfeld. Next to him is his mother. I don't remember her name because she was remarried. And I don't remember her name. And next to her is her second husband. The two people on the end I don't know. They were some second aunts and uncles. I'm not going to describe all the people, but they were all related, either cousins or aunts and uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the front row, the children are-- his name was [INAUDIBLE] Yakubovich. He was the bride's brother. By the way, you did an interview with his brother, [? Fari ?], who was on the other-- Frank-- who's on the other end. Did you do that interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two girls on the two sides I don't know. And in the middle is my little sister, Klara. Those were cousins and relatives. The picture-- by the way, the wedding was in Uzhorod, in Czechoslovakia.
